content.problemStatement:
You are given an array arrarrarr containing nnn integers. Your task is to return the **majority element**.

A majority element is defined as the element that occurs **more than ⌊n/2⌋⌊n / 2⌋⌊n/2⌋ times**.
It is guaranteed that such an element always exists.

⌊n/2⌋⌊n / 2⌋⌊n/2⌋ => The floor value means if 5 divided by 2 equals 2.5, we’ll choose 2 as the output because of the floor function. 

Follow-up:Can you solve this problem in **O(n)** time complexity while using only **O(1) extra space**?

**Function Declaration****Function Name**majorityElementmajorityElementmajorityElement – Finds the element that appears more than ⌊n/2⌋⌊n / 2⌋⌊n/2⌋ times in the array.

**Parameters**
arrarrarr : A list/array of integers of size nnn.
**Return Value**
Returns an **integer** — the majority element that appears more than half the time in the array.
Constraints:
n==arr.lengthn == arr.lengthn==arr.length
1≤n≤50,0001 \leq n \leq 50,0001≤n≤50,000
−109≤arr[i]≤109-10^9 \leq arr[i] \leq 10^9−109≤arr[i]≤109

Input Format


TTT → number of test cases




For each test case:



Line 1 → nnn (array size)
Line 2 → **n integers** representing the array




Output Format
One value per test case.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the majority element (the element that appears more than ⌊N/2⌋ times).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: N=6, element 7 appears 4 times (>⌊6/2⌋=3) so output 7. Test case 2: N=3, element 5 appears 2 times (>⌊3/2⌋=1) so output 5.
N=5, floor(5/2)=2. -1 appears 3 times, which is more than 2, therefore the majority element is -1.
N=8, floor(8/2)=4. The number 2 occurs 5 times (>4), so the answer is 2.

solution.approach: The problem guarantees that a majority element (appearing more than floor(N/2) times) exists. The Boyer‑Moore voting algorithm finds this element in a single pass using O(1) extra space. It maintains a candidate and a counter. When the current element equals the candidate, the counter is incremented; otherwise it is decremented. When the counter reaches zero, the current element becomes the new candidate. After the first pass, the candidate is the majority element.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(N_total) for all test cases combined.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (apart from input storage).
